{{Short description|Facial expression}}
{{Redirect|Scowl}}
{{Redirect|Sad face|an episode of the animated series ''Adventure Time''|Sad Face (Adventure Time)}}
[[File:Albrecht_Dürer_-_Bildnis_eines_unbekannten_Mannes.jpg|thumb|In his 16th-century portrait of an unidentified man, [[Albrecht Dürer]] powerfully captures personality through [[facial expression]], [[body language]], and clothing. Work: ''Bildnis eines unbekannten Mannes'' (1521)]]

A '''frown''' (also known as a '''scowl''') is a [[facial expression]] in which the [[eyebrows]] are brought together, and the forehead is wrinkled, usually indicating [[displeasure]], [[sadness]] or [[worry]], or less often [[confusion]] or [[Attentional control|concentration]].
The appearance of a frown varies by culture. An alternative usage in [[North America]] is thought of as an expression of the [[mouth]]. In those cases when used iconically, as with an emoticon, it is entirely presented by the curve of the lips forming a down-open curve. The mouth expression is also commonly referred to in the colloquial English phrase, especially in the United States, to "turn that frown upside down" which indicates changing from sad to happy.

==Description==
[[Charles Darwin]] described the primary act of frowning as the furrowing of the brow which leads to a rise in the upper lip and a down-turning of the corners of the mouth.<ref name=Darwin1872>{{cite book|last=Darwin|first=Charles R.|title=The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals|year=1872|publisher=John Murray|location=London|pages=148–152|title-link=The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals}}</ref> While the appearance of a frown varies from culture to culture, there appears to be some degree of universality to the recognition of the frown as a negative facial expression.<ref name=Russell92>{{Cite journal|last1=Russell|first1=J.A.|title=Is there universal recognition of emotion from facial expressions? A review of the cross-cultural studies|doi =10.1037/0033-2909.115.1.102|journal=[[Psychological Bulletin]]|volume=115|issue=1|pages=102–141|year=1994|pmid=8202574|s2cid=4823006 }}</ref> In fact, frowning as a component of anger or disgust is widely recognized as a universal expression easily recognized across cultures.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Ekman |first=Paul |title=Emotions revealed : recognizing faces and feelings to improve communication and emotional life |date=2004 |publisher=Henry Holt and Co |isbn=0-8050-7516-X |edition=1 |location=New York, N.Y. |pages=63 |oclc=54433903}}</ref> This universality suggests a shared adaptive quality to frowning allowing for social communication of [[negative emotion]]al states.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Izard, Carroll E.|author-link=Carroll Izard|title=Innate and Universal Facial Expressions: Evidence From Developmental and Cross-Cultural Research|journal=Psychological Bulletin|year=1994|volume=115|issue=2|pages=288–299|doi=10.1037/0033-2909.115.2.288|pmid=8165273}}</ref>

[[File:Illustration The Expression of Emotions in Man and Animals by Charles Darwin.jpg|thumb|left|alt=Photographs of frowning children from Darwin's The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals|Photographs of frowning and crying children from ''[[The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals]]'' (1872) by [[Charles Darwin]]]]

[[Scott Fahlman]] first suggested the use of the colon with the left parenthesis to iconically represent a frowning face on the Internet in what has become a well-known [[emoticon]].<ref>{{Citation|title=-) turns 25 |publisher=Associated Press |date=2007-09-20 |url=http://www.cnn.com/2007/TECH/09/18/emoticon.anniversary.ap/index.html |access-date=2007-09-20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071012051803/http://www.cnn.com/2007/TECH/09/18/emoticon.anniversary.ap/index.html |archive-date=2007-10-12 |url-status=dead }}</ref> In this form the frown is entirely presented as a curve of the lips facing away from the eyes.
Specifically, frowns that incorporate the furrowing of the brow are a response to perceived obstacles to the achievement of goals, while frowns that involve movement of the cheeks reflect an unpleasant reaction.<ref name=Pope94>{{Cite journal|last1=Pope|first1=L.|last2=Smith|first2=C.|doi=10.1080/02699939408408929|title=On the distinct meanings of smiles and frowns|journal=Cognition & Emotion|volume=8|pages=65–72|year=1994}}</ref> Just as smiling alone can make one feel better,<ref name=Strack88>{{cite journal|last=Strack|first=Fritz|author2=Martin, L. |author3=Stepper, S. |title=Inhibiting and facilitating conditions of the human smile: A non-obtrusive test of the facial feedback hypothesis|journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]|year=1988|volume=54|issue=5|pages=768–777|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.54.5.768 |pmid=3379579|s2cid=15291233 }}</ref> frowning can make one feel worse. In a scientific study participants who held their faces in a frown ranked images as more unpleasant than participants who viewed the images with a neutral facial expression.<ref name=Larsen92>{{Cite journal|last1=Larsen|first1=R.|last2=Kasimatis|first2=M.|last3=Frey|first3=K.|doi=10.1080/02699939208409689|pmid=29022461|title=Facilitating the Furrowed Brow: An Unobtrusive Test of the Facial Feedback Hypothesis Applied to Unpleasant Affect|journal=Cognition & Emotion|volume=6|issue=5|pages=321–338|year=1992}}</ref> In a similar test, participants reported increased anger with the manipulated expression of a frown and they also ranked cartoons they saw as less funny than participants with the manipulated expression of a smile.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Smith|first=Craig A.|title=Dimensions of appraisal and physiological response in emotion|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=Mar 1989|volume=56|issue=3|pages=339–353|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.56.3.339|pmid=2926633 }}</ref>

==Social behavior==

[[File:Mark Pearson Frown.png|thumb|An example of a spontaneous frown used to express displeasure.]]
In social settings frowns are most frequently used to express a range of negative emotions including anger and displeasure. However there are social differences that dictate who is allowed to frown in any given social setting. Socially dominant individuals are more likely to frown than those of a lower social status. Individuals with a high social status are permitted to display their emotions more freely, while low dominance individuals are expected to show signs of [[Tend and befriend|affiliation]].
<ref>{{cite book|last=LaFrance|first=M.|author2=Hect, M.A.|editor=P.Phillipot |editor2=R.S. Feldman |editor3=E.J. Coars|title=The social context of nonverbal behavior|year=1999|publisher=Cambridge University Press|location= Cambridge, UK|isbn=978-0-521-58666-5|pages=45–70|chapter=Option or obligation to smile: The effects of power and gender on facial expression.}}</ref> It is considered more socially acceptable for men to display negative emotions than women; women also tend to be less likely to frown than men.<ref name=Hess1999>{{cite journal|last=Hess|first=Ursula|author2=Adams, R.B.|author3=Kleck, R.E.|title=Who may frown and who should smile? Dominance, affiliation, and the display of happiness and anger|journal=Cognition and Emotion|year=2005|volume=19|issue=4|pages=515–536|doi=10.1080/02699930441000364|s2cid=18739720|url=http://www.psychophysiolab.com/uhess/pubs/HAK05.pdf|access-date=2011-08-26|archive-date=2012-03-31|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120331070144/http://www.psychophysiolab.com/uhess/pubs/HAK05.pdf|url-status=usurped}}</ref> Unlike [[smiling]], frowns do not appear to be directly socially [[wikt:contagious|contagious]]; seeing someone frown does not necessarily make you frown.<ref name=Hinsz91>{{Cite journal|last1=Hinsz|first1=V.B.|last2=Tomhave|first2=J.A.|s2cid=33343894|doi=10.1177/0146167291175014| title=Smile and (Half) the World Smiles with You, Frown and You Frown Alone| journal=[[Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin]]|volume=17|issue=5|pages=586–592|year=1991
}}</ref> However, in a scientific study participants that were subliminally exposed to frowning faces, were more likely to react with a frown when later shown a neutral face.<ref name=Ulf2000>{{cite journal|last=Ulf|first=Dimberg|author2=Thunberg, M. |author3=Elmehed, K. |title=Unconscious Facial Reactions to Emotional Facial Expressions|journal=[[Psychological Science (journal)|Psychological Science]]|date=January 2000|volume=11|issue=1|pages=86–89|doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00221|pmid=11228851|s2cid=67858 |citeseerx=10.1.1.508.2004}}</ref> Frowns can also increase counter-empathy reactions. When the loser of a competition frowns it increases brain responses associated with pleasure in the winner, which is possibly related to the feeling of [[schadenfreude]] experienced when witnessing the misfortune of others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Yamada|first1=M.|last2=Lamm|first2=C.|last3=Decety|first3=J.| doi=10.1037/a0023854|pmid=21728414|title=Pleasing frowns, disappointing smiles: An ERP investigation of counterempathy|journal=[[Emotion (journal)|Emotion]]|year=2011|volume=11|issue=6|pages=1336–1345}}</ref> Frowns also contribute to impressions formed even during brief encounters with other individuals. When shown a 30-second video clip (without audio) of a frowning college professor, students tend to rate the professor poorly on evaluations.<ref name=Ambady93>{{cite journal|last=Ambady|first=Nalini|author2=Rosenthal, R.|title=Half a Minute: Predicting Teacher Evaluations From Thin Slices of Nonverbal Behavior and Physical Attractiveness|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=1993|volume=64|issue=3|pages=431–441|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.64.3.431|s2cid=16122351 }}</ref>

==Muscles used==
[[File:Lateral head anatomy.jpg|thumb|Illustration of [[facial muscles]] and other tissue of the lateral [[human head]] and [[neck]]]]
It is a long-held belief that it takes more muscles to frown than it does to smile.<ref>{{cite book |last=Hix |first=John |title=Strange As It Seems |year=1931 |publisher=Sears Publishing Company |location=New York |page=224}}</ref> It is difficult to determine exactly how many muscles are involved in smiling or frowning as there is a wide range of facial expressions that might be considered a frown or a smile. At minimum ten muscles are required to smile in which only the upper lip and corners of the mouth are lifted.<ref name=DiscoveryHealth>{{cite web |last=Scheve |first=Tom |title=How many muscles does it take to smile? |date=2 June 2009 |url=http://health.howstuffworks.com/mental-health/human-nature/happiness/muscles-smile1.htm |publisher=[[Discovery Fit & Health]] |access-date=29 August 2011}}</ref> A similarly minimal frown requires only six muscles to lower the corners of the mouth.<ref name=DiscoveryHealth /> According to plastic surgeon Dr. David H. Song of the [[University of Chicago Medical Center]], however, frowning requires 11 muscles while smiling requires 12.<ref name=song>{{cite web |last=Adams |first=Cecil |title=Does it take fewer muscles to smile than it does to frown? |url=http://www.straightdope.com/columns/read/2489/does-it-take-fewer-muscles-to-smile-than-it-does-to-frown |work=The Straight Dope |date=16 January 2004 |access-date=26 August 2011}}</ref> This method of counting the number of muscles used in generating a facial expression does not take into account the energy consumed by each muscle or the individual variability in facial muscles. While humans share facial musculature to express the universal emotions, some humans have more muscles in their faces and may use more of them when smiling or frowning.<ref name=Waller08>{{cite journal|last=Waller|first=Bridget M.|author2=Cray, James J. |author3=Burrows, Anne M. |title=Selection for Universal Facial Emotion|journal=Emotion|year=2008|volume=8|issue=3|pages=435–439|doi=10.1037/1528-3542.8.3.435|pmid=18540761|citeseerx=10.1.1.612.9868}}</ref>

{| class="wikitable"
|+|'''Muscles of facial expression<ref name=song />'''
|-
! Frowning !! Smiling
|-
| [[Platysma]] (2 muscles) || [[Zygomaticus major]] (2 muscles)
|-
| [[Orbicularis oculi]] (2 muscles) || Orbicularis oculi (2 muscles)
|-
| [[Corrugator supercilii]] (2 muscles) || [[Levator labii superioris]] (2 muscles)
|-
| [[Procerus]] (1 muscle) || [[Levator anguli oris]] (2 muscles)
|-
| [[Orbicularis oris]] (1 muscle) || [[Risorius]] (2 muscles)
|-
| [[Mentalis]] (1 muscle) || [[Zygomaticus minor]] (2 muscles)
|-
| [[Depressor anguli oris]] (2 muscles) ||
|-
| 11 muscles total || 12 muscles total
|}
